name: Xan
description: Xan is a ghost from Azerbaijan folklore. Azerbaijani ghost of a murdered person or wronged lover. Haunts specific locations until justice served or bones buried properly. Traditional belief in restless dead. Importance of proper burial rites. It is said to dwell in Scene of death, family home, grave sites.
appearance: Azerbaijani ghost of a murdered person or wronged lover.
behavior: Xan is night, appear, haunt in Azerbaijan folklore.
habitat: Scene of death, family home, grave sites
cultural_significance: Traditional belief in restless dead. Importance of proper burial rites.
summary: Azerbaijani ghost of a murdered person or wronged lover
fun_fact: Azerbaijani ghost of a murdered person or wronged lover. Haunts specific locations until justice served or bones buried properly.
seo_description: Azerbaijani ghost of a murdered person or wronged lover. Haunts specific locations until justice served or bones buried properly.
source: Azerbaijani folklore; the Xan is a ghost of a murdered person.

name: Xana
description: Xana is a water-spirit from Spain folklore. Documented by Ramón Menéndez Pidal. Connected to pre-Roman Celtic beliefs and the cult of Diana. Name derives from Diana/Jana. It is known to be guard, benevolent, lure, enchant, vengeful. It is said to dwell in Streams, rivers, caves (cuevas de xanas).
appearance: Xana takes the form of a water-spirit known in Spain folklore.
behavior: Xana is guard, benevolent, lure, enchant, vengeful in Spain folklore.
cultural_significance: Documented by Ramón Menéndez Pidal. Connected to pre-Roman Celtic beliefs and the cult of Diana. Name derives from Diana/Jana.
summary: Water nymphs of Asturian folklore, associated with streams and caverns
fun_fact: Documented by Ramón Menéndez Pidal. Connected to pre-Roman Celtic beliefs and the cult of Diana. Name derives from Diana/Jana.
seo_description: Water nymphs of Asturian folklore, associated with streams and caverns. Connected to the cult of Diana. Beautiful female figures with enchanting songs
source: Asturian folklore; Xanas are water nymphs associated with caves and springs.

source_references.t: Xana: Fountain Fairy of Asturian Folklore

name: Xbalanque (Jaguar Sun)
description: One of the Maya Hero Twins from the Popol Vuh. Along with his brother Hunahpu, he descended into Xibalba (the underworld) and defeated the Lords of Death, becoming the sun. Represents triumph of intelligence over brute force.
appearance: Often depicted wearing jaguar mask or headdress. Youthful, dynamic figure with body paint. In jaguar form: powerful spotted cat with glowing eyes. Carries magical weapons and implements from the Popol Vuh.
behavior: Xbalanque (Jaguar Sun) is transform in Guatemala folklore.
cultural_significance: Central figure in Maya mythology and the Popol Vuh. Symbolizes the cyclic nature of death and rebirth, duality of light and darkness. Their story explains the sun's daily journey and the victory of life over death.
summary: Xbalanque (Jaguar Sun) is a hero from the folklore and mythology of Guatemala.
fun_fact: One of the Maya Hero Twins from the Popol Vuh. Along with his brother Hunahpu, he descended into Xibalba (the underworld) and defeated the Lords of De
seo_description: One of the Maya Hero Twins from the Popol Vuh. Along with his brother Hunahpu, he descended into Xibalba (the underworld) and defeated the Lords of De
source: Maya mythology; Xbalanque is one of the Hero Twins who defeated the lords of Xibalba.

name: Xibalba
description: Xibalba is a spirit from Guatemala folklore. Maya underworld of the dead. Ruled by Lords of Death. Journey through Xibalba in Popol Vuh hero twins story. Place of tests and trials. Maya underworld. Popol Vuh connection.
appearance: Typical appearance matches its role as a restless and protective and mischievous being.
behavior: Xibalba is restless, protective, mischievous in Guatemala folklore.
summary: Xibalba is a spirit from the folklore and mythology of Guatemala.
fun_fact: Maya underworld of the dead. Ruled by Lords of Death. Journey through Xibalba in Popol Vuh hero twins story. Place of tests and trials.
seo_description: Maya underworld of the dead. Ruled by Lords of Death. Journey through Xibalba in Popol Vuh hero twins story. Place of tests and trials.
source: Maya mythology; Xibalba is the underworld realm of fear and death.

name: Xjabil
description: Xjabil is a guardian from Guatemala folklore. Guatemalan Maya dwarf. Mountain helper. It is known to be protective, territorial, vigilant. It is said to dwell in High mountain peaks and alpine regions of Guatemala.
appearance: Typical appearance matches its role as a protective and territorial and vigilant being.
behavior: Xjabil is protective, territorial, vigilant in Guatemala folklore.
habitat: High mountain peaks and alpine regions of Guatemala
summary: Xjabil is a guardian from the folklore and mythology of Guatemala.
fun_fact: Known for loyalty and stone manipulation and limited range.
seo_description: Maya dwarf spirit from highland folklore. Tiny old men living in mountains who help virtuous people find lost objects.
source: Guatemalan Maya folklore — guardian spirit from Guatemalan oral tradition.

name: Xnifs
description: Xnifs is a goblin from Malta folklore. Small mischievous creatures living in ruins and abandoned places. Cause minor household troubles and steal food. Maltese imps. Minor household spirits. It is said to dwell in Ruins, abandoned places.
appearance: Small mischievous creatures living in ruins and abandoned places. Cause minor household troubles and steal food. Minor household spirits.

description: Xolotl is a god from Mexico folklore. Aztec dog-headed god of death, guides souls to underworld. Aztec psychopomp. It is said to dwell in Underworld, associated with sunset.

name: Xác
description: Xác is a zombie from Vietnam folklore. Vietnamese zombie-like creature, corpse reanimated by black magic or improper burial. Moves slowly, feeds on living flesh. Can be killed by decapitati. Zombie concept from Chinese and Western influence. Fear of premature burial. It is said to dwell in Graveyards, abandoned villages.
appearance: Vietnamese zombie-like creature, corpse reanimated by black magic or improper burial.
behavior: Vietnamese zombie-like creature, corpse reanimated by black magic or improper burial. Can be killed by decapitati. It is said to dwell in Graveyards, abandoned villages.
cultural_significance: Zombie concept from Chinese and Western influence. Fear of premature burial.

name: Xtabai
description: Xtabai (also spelled Xtabay) is a demonic femme fatale from Yucatec Maya folklore who lures men to their deaths with her incredible beauty. She appears as a stunning woman with long black hair falling to her ankles, wearing a white dress, and combs her hair with the spines of a Tzacam cactus while waiting behind a ceiba tree. According to legend, she was once Utz-colel, a haughty and pure woman who, after death, prayed to evil spirits and became a demon. She lures men deep into the forest, seduces them, then transforms into a poisonous serpent and devours them. In some versions she throws them off cliffs or rips out their hearts. The ceiba tree — sacred in Maya culture as the axis mundi connecting heaven, earth, and underworld — becomes her hunting ground.
appearance: Beautiful woman with long, shining black hair to her ankles, large dark eyes, and a flowing white dress. From behind she appears perfect, but her face reveals decay or skull-like features. Hides behind ceiba trees, combing her hair with cactus spines.
behavior: Seductive predator that lures men at night with her beauty. Leads them deep into the forest, disorients them, then kills them. Transforms into a serpent to devour her victims. Uses the sacred ceiba tree as her ambush point. Active during late night hours.
habitat: Forests, ceiba trees, rural roads, cliffs, and gorges throughout Belize and the Yucatán Peninsula
cultural_significance: Xtabai represents the blending of pre-Columbian Maya beliefs with Spanish colonial morality tales. She serves as a warning against infidelity, temptation, and wandering alone at night. Her legend has inspired art, literature, and television across the Yucatán region.
summary: Demon woman from Maya folklore who lures men with her beauty, then transforms into a serpent to devour them
fun_fact: Xtabai represents the blending of pre-Columbian Maya beliefs with Spanish colonial morality tales. She serves as a warning against infidelity, temptation, and wandering alone at night. Her legend has inspired art, literature, and television across the Yucatán region.
seo_description: Xtabai (also spelled Xtabay) is a demonic femme fatale from Yucatec Maya folklore who lures men to their deaths with her incredible beauty. She appears as ...
source: Belizean oral tradition; snip Wikipedia; snip GrisDismation Legends of Belize

name: ǐXue
description: ǐXue is the trickster being of the ǓKun (ǓXung) people of northeastern Namibia, described by scholars as Khoisan mythology's most unrestrained shapeshifter with a trigger-sharp readiness for transformation. ǐXue can transform himself into each and everything conceivable. He has a complicated association with the moon and the hare (one of his most integral animal personas), and is connected to the divinity Huwe and spirit beings ǁgana. ǐXue appears in over 110 stories recorded by Lucy Lloyd from two young ǓKun storytellers between 1879 and 1882. The trickster spirit also engages in a complex dispute with Tamme involving their respective wives.
appearance: Trigger-shape transformer appearing as a hare, various animals, or human. Highly variable and mutable form.
behavior: Uncontrollable impulsive shapeshifter. Engages in endless transformations. Associated with lunar cycles and hare mythology. Participates in foundational myths about human mortality.
habitat: North-eastern Namibia (ǓKun territory), Kalahari Desert region
cultural_significance: ǐXue represents the San philosophical understanding of the fluidity of identity, the relationship between humans and animals, and the origin of death.
summary: The ultimate unrestrained shapeshifter of the ǓKun San people of northeastern Namibia, associated with the moon and hare
fun_fact: ǐXue is the unrestrained trickster shapeshifter of the ǓKun San people of northeastern Namibia, capable of transforming into everything imaginable.
seo_title: ǐXue – Namibia's Ultimate Shapeshifter in San Mythology
seo_description: ǐXue is the unrestrained trickster shapeshifter of the ǓKun San people of northeastern Namibia, capable of transforming into everything imaginable.
source: ǓKun oral tradition; snip snip Bleek and Lloyd archive; snip snip Mathias Guenther ethnographic studies

source_references.t: ǐXue: Lightning Bird of San Mythology

name: Xiezhi
description: A mythical creature capable of distinguishing between truth and lies, often used as a symbol of justice. It is commonly found in Celestial courts, sacred forests. Associated behaviors include truth-seeking, judgmental.
appearance: A goat-like or lion-like creature with a single horn on its forehead.
behavior: When witnessing a dispute, it instinctively gores the party that is lying or guilty with its horn.
cultural_significance: Appears on the robes of ancient Chinese judges to signify their impartiality.
summary: Xiezhi is a beast from the folklore and mythology of China.
fun_fact: Appears on the robes of ancient Chinese judges to signify their impartiality.
seo_description: The Xiezhi: the divine judge of Chinese mythology. A creature that knows the truth.

name: Xhindet
description: Xhindet is a spirit from Albania folklore. Also known as xhind. It is said to dwell in Mountain passes, old ruins. In folklore, it is described as play pranks, hide objects, and lead travelers astray in the mountains. Reminders that the world is full of unseen forces that require wit to navigate.
appearance: Ethereal, shifting forms; often appearing as strangers or animals.
behavior: Play pranks, hide objects, and lead travelers astray in the mountains.
cultural_significance: Reminders that the world is full of unseen forces that require wit to navigate.
summary: Xhindet is a spirit from Albanian folklore in Albania, believed to embody the spiritual traditions of the region.
fun_fact: Reminders that the world is full of unseen forces that require wit to navigate.
seo_description: The Xhindet: the mischievous trickster spirits of Albanian folklore.
